trust_assumption: A site assumes whatever a crawler indexes is content it intended to publish, so anything reachable by URL is fair game for public search.
violation: Crafted operator queries surface admin panels, config files, and listings the owner never meant to be discoverable, turning the index into a vulnerability catalog.

trust_assumption: A public repository is trusted to contain no live secrets because developers believe deleting a file or rotating a key removes it from history.
violation: Commit history and forks are mined for still-valid credentials, tokens, and keys whose forgeable 'it was removed' provenance never actually revoked them.

trust_assumption: An authentication system trusts a username/password pair as proof of identity regardless of where that pair has previously appeared.
violation: Reused credentials from prior breach dumps and pastes are replayed against the target, the leaked-elsewhere origin laundered into a valid login.

trust_assumption: A misconfigured DNS server trusts any resolver that asks to be a secondary, serving the entire zone via AXFR as if to a peer.
violation: An arbitrary external client requests a zone transfer and receives the full internal hostname inventory the zone was never meant to hand to strangers.

trust_assumption: Registration records, historical DNS, and certificate-transparency logs are treated as benign public bookkeeping rather than a map of the organization's footprint.
violation: Aggregated WHOIS history, passive DNS, and subdomain enumeration reconstruct hidden and forgotten assets the owner assumed were obscure.

trust_assumption: An owner trusts that removing or changing content takes it offline, unaware that crawlers and archives preserve a forgeable-immutable snapshot of the old state.
violation: Archived pages, old endpoints, and crawled link graphs resurface deleted parameters, paths, and data the live site no longer exposes.

trust_assumption: A physical premises trusts that proximity and visibility to outsiders reveal nothing actionable about badges, layouts, wireless, or staff routines.
violation: On-site observation captures badge formats, entry points, AP signals, and tailgating opportunities that a casual presence was assumed unable to gather.

trust_assumption: Active Directory trusts any authenticated domain user to read the directory broadly enough to compute every ACL, group, and session relationship for usability.
violation: A single low-privilege account derives the complete attack-path graph and shortest route to Domain Admin the directory never meant any one principal to see whole.

trust_assumption: A flat internal network trusts that anything already inside the perimeter is allowed to reach and probe every other host and port.
violation: A single foothold sweeps the internal address space and service ports, mapping reachable targets the segmentation model assumed it could not enumerate.

techniques.name: Internal network scanning (Nmap/masscan host & port discovery)

trust_assumption: An SMB/RPC service trusts a null or low-privilege session to list shares, users, and policies because that enumeration is treated as ordinary file-browsing convenience.
violation: A near-anonymous session inventories shares, accounts, and ACLs across hosts, exposing the data and identity layout the server assumed required real authorization.

trust_assumption: Cloud control-plane APIs trust any valid principal to enumerate IAM, resource inventory, and network topology because read/list calls are treated as harmless management operations.
violation: A foothold credential lists the entire account's roles, trust policies, and misconfigurations, revealing privilege-escalation paths the IAM model assumed stayed compartmentalized.

trust_assumption: An operating system trusts any code already running in a user session to enumerate local processes, services, and accounts as routine self-inspection.
violation: An attacker's in-session process profiles running software, privileged services, and logged-on users to plan escalation the OS treats as benign introspection.

trust_assumption: File shares and mailboxes trust an authenticated principal to bulk-read and stage their contents because access control is assumed to bound what gets copied, not the act of copying.
violation: A foothold sweeps shares and mailboxes wholesale into a staging area, exfiltrating documents and correspondence at a scale the per-file permission model never anticipated.

trust_assumption: Databases, source repositories, and cloud object stores trust a credentialed reader to export them in full because read access is conflated with authorized bulk extraction.
violation: A single valid grant dumps entire databases, code repos, and storage buckets, copying out the whole datastore the access model assumed would only be touched record-by-record.

trust_assumption: A desktop session trusts any process running within it to read the framebuffer and clipboard, since on-screen and copied data are assumed visible only to the legitimate user.
violation: Malicious in-session code grabs screenshots and clipboard contents, capturing credentials and sensitive data the windowing model never isolated from co-resident processes.
